title: A comprehensive method for quantifying human cytomegalovirus plaque assays
author: Kelnhofer-Millevolte, Laurel E.

steps.title: The following sections include detailed steps...
steps.instruction: The following sections include detailed steps for both setting up hCMV plaque assays and subsequent imaging and analysis of fixed plaques. Many labs have established protocols that reliably generate reproducible plaques. Minor differences are unlikely to impact final data collection. Those familiar with hCMV plaque assays may proceed directly to step 3.6 for imaging and processing.

steps.title: Plate experiment as desired, for example,...
steps.instruction: Plate experiment as desired, for example, siRNA or inhibitor treatment as in Kelnhofer-Millevolte et al. (2025) and Shin and Kim (2026).

steps.title: For experimental infections, dilute the virus...
steps.instruction: For experimental infections, dilute the virus to the desired MOI in serum-free media. MOI is based on experimental question; for our experiments, we use an MOI of 1. Incubate the viral inoculum with cells for 1 h, with shaking every 10 min to ensure infection. If the lab has a rocker or similar machine in cell incubators, it can be used here. If not, periodic rocking of plates by hand is sufficient. After this 1 h, replace the viral inoculum with 4%-FBS-containing media (see the recipe in Section 6.4) as per Table 2 to allow for an adequate volume of supernatant at harvest. For the Towne strain, we recommend supernatant harvest between 4 and 6 days post-infection.

steps.title: Recommended inoculum volumes for infection
steps.instruction: Recommended inoculum volumes for infection.

steps.title: Recommended volume of media to be used in section 3
steps.instruction: Recommended volume of media to be used in section 3.1 based on experiment size.

steps.title: Transfer the supernatant volume into a 15-mL centrifuge tube
steps.instruction: Transfer the supernatant volume into a 15-mL centrifuge tube. Note: For larger experiments, the supernatant can be collected into multiple 15-mL centrifuge tubes or 50-mL centrifuge tubes as needed.

steps.title: Centrifuge at 3,000g for 2 min to remove cellular debris
steps.instruction: Centrifuge at 3,000g for 2 min to remove cellular debris.

steps.title: Aliquot into 1-mL volumes using cryovials or microcentrifuge tubes
steps.instruction: Aliquot into 1-mL volumes using cryovials or microcentrifuge tubes.

steps.title: Flash-freeze the cryovials in liquid nitrogen...
steps.instruction: Flash-freeze the cryovials in liquid nitrogen (or a slurry of dry ice with 100% ethanol) before storing the aliquots in -80°C, or proceed directly to dilution (step 3.3).

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: Dry ice – ethanol slurry takes longer to freeze harvested pellets. Additionally, if choosing this mixture, it will need to be made ahead of time to ensure that the samples are preserved properly.

steps.title: We recommend using six-well plates rather...
steps.instruction: We recommend using six-well plates rather than 12-well plates for hCMV plaque assays because they result in less variation between wells. Furthermore, we recommend at least three serial dilutions per condition to be tested in triplicate as described below. We have listed an example of an infection below, with the Towne strain on HFF-T cells which require DMEM. Adjust the cell line and media according to Table 1 depending on the hCMV strain.

steps.title: Day 0:
steps.instruction: Day 0:

steps.title: Aspirate media from HFF-T cells
steps.instruction: Aspirate media from HFF-T cells. Wash two times with 10 mL of 1× PBS, aspirating after each wash. Add 3 mL trypsin to the plate. Incubate the plate at 37°C for 5–10 min.

steps.title: Use 9 mL of DMEM with...
steps.instruction: Use 9 mL of DMEM with 10% FBS and gently triturate HFF-T cells to lift them from the plate. Transfer the cells and media to a 15-mL centrifuge tube. Centrifuge at 500g for 5 min.

steps.title: Aspirate supernatant; be careful not to disturb the cell pellet
steps.instruction: Aspirate supernatant; be careful not to disturb the cell pellet. Resuspend the cell pellet in 10 mL DMEM with 10% FBS. Count the cells using a hemocytometer for fibroblasts as automated cell counters are less reliable with large cells such as fibroblasts.

steps.title: Dilute the HFF-T cells to ~210,000...
steps.instruction: Dilute the HFF-T cells to ~210,000 cells/2 mL using DMEM with 10% FBS. Ensure that the cells are well distributed in the media. Plate 2 mL of cell/media mixture per well. Shake the plate along the X and Y axes to distribute the cells uniformly.

steps.title: Incubate the plates overnight at 37°C
steps.instruction: Incubate the plates overnight at 37°C.

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: Prior to preparing the viral dilutions, check that the cells plated in step 3.2 are 100% confluent. If the cells are not confluent, wait another day for the cells to grow before starting the plaque assay. Additionally, even a single freeze–thaw cycle of hCMV can reduce the titers of the virus by over 10-fold and will decrease the reproducibility of the assay. It is critical to not thaw the virus until the cells are ready for infection. The recommended dilutions for the Towne strain are 10−2 to 10−4 for high-MOI initial experiments and undiluted to 10−2 for low-MOI initial experiments. Common high-MOI experiments for hCMV range from 1 to 3, and for low MOI, this ranges from 0.1 to 0.

steps.title: Day 1:
steps.instruction: Day 1:

steps.title: Thaw the viral aliquot with an...
steps.instruction: Thaw the viral aliquot with an unknown titer in 37°C water bath. Note: If conducting a plaque assay for a viral stock, we recommend a brief water bath sonication (1 min on, 1 min off to rest, 1 min on) of the samples to ensure the release of any virus that is still cell-associated.

steps.title: Label the microcentrifuge and 15-mL centrifuge...
steps.instruction: Label the microcentrifuge and 15-mL centrifuge tubes with dilution and conditions. Add 3.6 mL of serum-free DMEM (see the recipe in Section 6.3) to each tube. For 10−1 dilution, start with 900 µL of serum-free DMEM.

steps.title: Vortex the viral aliquot for 30 s
steps.instruction: Vortex the viral aliquot for 30 s. Gently pipet up and down at least five times prior to the transfer of the inoculum. This is important to coat the pipet tip and ensure the accurate transfer of the virus.

steps.title: Transfer 100 µL of the supernatant...
steps.instruction: Transfer 100 µL of the supernatant from the experimental condition into the 10−1 vial. Discard the pipet tip.

steps.title: Vortex the dilution for 30 s
steps.instruction: Vortex the dilution for 30 s. Using a fresh pipet tip, triturate three times.

steps.title: Transfer 400 µL into the next dilution tube
steps.instruction: Transfer 400 µL into the next dilution tube. Discard the pipet tip.

steps.title: Repeat steps 5 and 6 until...
steps.instruction: Repeat steps 5 and 6 until the end of the desired dilution series (Figure 2).

steps.title: Schematic of standard dilution series for...
steps.instruction: Schematic of standard dilution series for hCMV plaque assays as described in Section 3.3.

steps.title: (Continued from Section 3
steps.instruction: (Continued from Section 3.3).

steps.title: 8) Remove the six-well plates from...
steps.instruction: 8) Remove the six-well plates from the incubator and aspirate media. Wash all wells with 1 mL of PBS each. Aspirate PBS. Take care not to disturb the monolayer.

steps.title: 9) Vortex each dilution sample for 10 s
steps.instruction: 9) Vortex each dilution sample for 10 s. Immediately after vortexing the sample, add 1 mL of the inoculum to each well. Dispense each dilution in three separate wells.

steps.title: 10) Incubate the plates for 1 h
steps.instruction: 10) Incubate the plates for 1 h. Agitate the plates every 10 min to encourage even viral coating of the entire monolayer.

steps.title: 11) Incubate the plates with inoculum on the cells overnight
steps.instruction: 11) Incubate the plates with inoculum on the cells overnight.

steps.title: Day 2:
steps.instruction: Day 2:

steps.title: Prepare the overlay in 50-mL centrifuge tube(s)
steps.instruction: Prepare the overlay in 50-mL centrifuge tube(s). Each well requires 2 mL of overlay. Pour ~15 mL 2% methylcellulose (see the recipe in Section 6.1) into the centrifuge tube first before gently pipetting 15 mL DMEM with 4% FBS on top.

steps.title: Warm the overlay in the 37°C...
steps.instruction: Warm the overlay in the 37°C water bath for a minimum of 30 min. Gently triturate the overlay several times, taking care not to shake and add bubbles.

steps.title: Remove the infected plates from the incubator
steps.instruction: Remove the infected plates from the incubator. Aspirate inoculum, taking care to use a fresh tip for each dilution.

steps.title: Wash each well with 1 mL...
steps.instruction: Wash each well with 1 mL of 1× PBS; aspirate the PBS.

steps.title: Gently pipet 2 mL of methylcellulose-based overlay into each well
steps.instruction: Gently pipet 2 mL of methylcellulose-based overlay into each well. Pipet slowly to limit the formation of bubbles. Supporting the tip of the pipet on the edge of the well also minimizes the disruption of the cell monolayer as overlay is added to the well. Avoid large bubbles.

steps.title: Halfway through the extended incubation period,...
steps.instruction: Halfway through the extended incubation period, gently layer an additional 1 mL of warmed DMEM with 4% FBS.

steps.title: When holding the plate up to...
steps.instruction: When holding the plate up to a light source, the plaques will be visible as white spots on the plate. If they are visible by eye, they are ready to fix. For the Towne strain, this occurs approximately at day

steps.title: For the average time to fix...
steps.instruction: For the average time to fix for other strains, refer to Table

steps.title: Once the timing has been optimized,...
steps.instruction: Once the timing has been optimized, keep consistent among all conditions for a most accurate plaque size quantification.

steps.title: Day 14:
steps.instruction: Day 14:

steps.title: Aspirate approximately half of the overlay
steps.instruction: Aspirate approximately half of the overlay. Do this by tilting the plates to ~60° while aspirating half of the side of the well. Note: This is recommended to reduce the disruption of the cell monolayer. Other labs have had success using less viscous material for overlay such as Avicel (Matrosovich et al., 2006), although we recommend using a clear overlay for the hCMV plaque assays, such as agarose or methylcellulose, to allow visualizing the cells during the assay. It is important to be able to view the cells over the long incubation period to monitor plaque formation.

steps.title: Gently add 1 mL of 1×...
steps.instruction: Gently add 1 mL of 1× PBS to each well, layering on top of the remaining overlay.

steps.title: Aspirate half of the PBS/inoculum using...
steps.instruction: Aspirate half of the PBS/inoculum using the same method as in step 1.

steps.title: Repeat steps 2 and 3 two more times
steps.instruction: Repeat steps 2 and 3 two more times. The overlay should be clear by the third wash. If not, repeat steps 2 and 3 two more times.

steps.title: Add 0
steps.instruction: Add 0.5 mL of 1% crystal violet (see the recipe in Section 6.2) to each well.

steps.title: Gently shake the plates to ensure...
steps.instruction: Gently shake the plates to ensure that the crystal violet spreads evenly and place on a low-speed orbital shaker at RT for 15 mins.

steps.title: Remove most of the crystal violet...
steps.instruction: Remove most of the crystal violet by flipping the six-well plate over a funnel into a waste tank. Tap the plate against the opening of the funnel until the crystal violet splatter no longer shows. Note: Ensure that the crystal violet is handled according to institutional safety protocols. This may require the removal of crystal violet and washing with deionized (DI) water using a pipet and moving the liquid directly into a disposal canister.

steps.title: Wash the wells repeatedly by dunking...
steps.instruction: Wash the wells repeatedly by dunking the plate right side up in a tub of DI water to minimize damage to the fixed monolayer. Wash the wells until the contrast of the plaques to the cell monolayer is easily visible.

steps.title: Place the six-well plates on a...
steps.instruction: Place the six-well plates on a bench pad upside down and at an angle to dry; this can be achieved by leaning the plate against the lid. Allow the plaques to dry at least overnight prior to imaging.

steps.title: Note
steps.instruction: Note: The imaging for this protocol was originally optimized in our previous paper (Kelnhofer-Millevolte et al., 2025) using Molecular Devices ImageXpress Micro high-content imaging system. Here we present multiple additional modalities to image the fixed plaque assays. Our goal is to make the protocol more accessible by providing many options for imaging based on equipment available in the lab. The advantages and disadvantages of each imaging modality are presented in the “Discussion”. Wells imaged for analysis should have 10–100 plaques and have minimal plaques touching other plaques. If no wells meet these criteria (see “Discussion” for troubleshooting). Wells only need to be imaged using one method. After images are gathered, proceed to Section 3.7 for image analysis. If the volume of plaques is not desired, the measurement ruler placement can be omitted.

steps.title: Imaging option 1—Confocal microscope:
steps.instruction: Imaging option 1—Confocal microscope:

steps.title: Place the fixed plates from Section 3
steps.instruction: Place the fixed plates from Section 3.5 with the plastic bottoms of wells facing the microscope objective without lid in the plate holder area. Here we use a Zeiss LSM 980 confocal microscope with a sample finder, but other comparable confocal microscopes can be used.

steps.title: Set the imaging setting to acquire...
steps.instruction: Set the imaging setting to acquire fluorescence images with a Cy5 (647-nm excitation) filter set. Collect images using a 10× objective.

steps.title: Open the acquisition tab and select “AI Sample Finder”
steps.instruction: Open the acquisition tab and select “AI Sample Finder”. Select the “Center Position” and “Find Sample buttons”. The AI Sample Finder will automatically identify the individual wells as regions of interest (ROIs) in the multiwell plate and generate an overview image with the wells highlighted.

steps.title: Select the “tiles” option from the main menu
steps.instruction: Select the “tiles” option from the main menu. In the tiles menu that opens, select “Show viewer”. Select “Setup from sample carrier” option for the tile type.

steps.title: Select “Fill Factor” located in the top menu
steps.instruction: Select “Fill Factor” located in the top menu. Set the fill factor to 100%, and then select 5) all wells from the carrier menu and select the + sign.

steps.title: Set the tile size to 850
steps.instruction: Set the tile size to 850.1 μm × 709.3 μm for a standard six-well plate. Activate tiles at 10% overlap.

steps.title: Add support points throughout the tile grid
steps.instruction: Add support points throughout the tile grid. Navigate to the “Tiles” menu then select “Focus Surface and Support Points” submenu. In the “Multiple Support Points” heading, enter four columns and four rows. Select all wells and then select “distribute”. In the “Interpolation Degree” subheading, choose “2 – Parabolic Saddle Surface (at least 9 support points)”.

steps.title: In the main menu, select “Live”
steps.instruction: In the main menu, select “Live”. Navigate to “Verify Support Points” heading and select “Verify”.

steps.title: Adjust the focus throughout the support point
steps.instruction: Adjust the focus throughout the support point. Then, select “Set Z and move to next” until all support points are verified.

steps.title: Under the “Options” subheading, input “10%”...
steps.instruction: Under the “Options” subheading, input “10%” for “Tile Overlap”, choose meander for “Travel in Tile Regions”, check “Tile Regions/Positions” box and choose “Sort by Y, then X”, check “Carrier Wells/Container” box and choose “Meander”.

steps.title: Click the “start experiment” button with...
steps.instruction: Click the “start experiment” button with the tiling parameters in place.

steps.title: Once capture is complete, navigate to...
steps.instruction: Once capture is complete, navigate to the “Processing” tab, and under the “Geometric” subheading, select “Stitching”. Under the “Parameters” subheading, select “Inplace”. Under “Channels” heading, select “All Individually”. Set the parameters as follows for edge detector—”yes”, 5% Minimal Overlap, 10% Max Shift, Optimized for Comparer, and Best for Global Optimizer. Navigate to the “Inputs” submenu and then select the newly generated image from the tile scan.

steps.title: Separate each well into an individual file for easier processing
steps.instruction: Separate each well into an individual file for easier processing. After the tiles have been stitched, open the “Processing” tab, and under “Utilities”, select “Split Scenes”. Select the stitched image of all of the wells together as “Input” and designate a folder to save the files in; then, select “Apply”.

steps.title: Export the files as
steps.instruction: Export the files as.czi.

steps.title: To open
steps.instruction: To open.czi in ImageJ, navigate to the Plugins tab and hover over the Bio-Formats option to reveal Bio-Formats Importer. From there, select the file you want to open. A menu will appear asking how these images should be opened. Check the “Use virtual stack” option under the “Memory Management” subheading and select “OK”. A new window will open asking which “Scenes” to open. These are different pixel sizes of the same image. Uncheck scene 1 and scroll down to check the last scene and open the file.

steps.title: Navigate to the Image tab and...
steps.instruction: Navigate to the Image tab and hover over Type and select 8-bit. To add a scale bar, navigate to Analyze and hover over Tools to select “Scalebar…”. Input the desired size. To save the file, navigate to File, hover over “Save as”, and choose “Jpeg…”.

steps.title: Proceed to image processing in Section 3
steps.instruction: Proceed to image processing in Section 3.7.

steps.title: Imaging option 2—Bio-Rad ChemiDoc™ MP Imaging System:
steps.instruction: Imaging option 2—Bio-Rad ChemiDoc™ MP Imaging System:

steps.title: Place the fixed plates from Section 3
steps.instruction: Place the fixed plates from Section 3.5 right side up without the lid in Bio-Rad ChemiDoc™ MP imaging system using the black background plate.

steps.title: Place a fluorescent metric ruler (commonly...
steps.instruction: Place a fluorescent metric ruler (commonly used for Bio-Rad calibration) along the edge of the six-well plate.

steps.title: Select Cy5
steps.instruction: Select Cy5.5 imaging setting and adjust the exposure to between 1 and 2 s of exposure or use the auto-exposure feature.

steps.title: Collect image
steps.instruction: Collect image. Proceed to image processing in Section 3.7.

steps.title: Imaging option 3—Smartphone camera:
steps.instruction: Imaging option 3—Smartphone camera:

steps.title: Place a white paper on a benchtop
steps.instruction: Place a white paper on a benchtop. Next, place the fixed plates from Section 3.5 upside-down on the paper. Alternatively, a light box such as those used to image Coomassie-stained gels can be used for better illumination.

steps.title: Place a metric ruler along the...
steps.instruction: Place a metric ruler along the edge of the 6-well plate if the plaque area is desired in the calculations.

steps.title: Hold the smartphone parallel to the...
steps.instruction: Hold the smartphone parallel to the plate and tap the screen to focus on the well of interest. Hold the phone to avoid shadowing on the well of interest.

steps.title: Collect image
steps.instruction: Collect image. Be sure to convert the photo file to .jpg file type if needed. Proceed to image processing in Section 3.7.

steps.title: Note that processing images will require...
steps.instruction: Note that processing images will require some optimization of the set to be processed. Once the parameters are optimized, apply the parameters to all wells imaged. This protocol was developed for hCMV plaques, which appear as darker areas of staining on a lighter background of cell monolayer.

steps.title: Open the image file in ImageJ
steps.instruction: Open the image file in ImageJ. Ensure that the file is an 8-bit by selecting “Image” tab, then under “Type” menu, click “8-bit.”.

steps.title: For images acquired on Bio-Rad ChemiDoc™...
steps.instruction: For images acquired on Bio-Rad ChemiDoc™ MP imaging system or smartphone, use the line tool to trace 1 cm along the top edge of the ruler. Then, in the “Analyze” tab, select “Set Scale”. The length of the traced line will auto-populate in pixels. Ensure that “Known distance” is set to 1 and “Unit of length” is set to cm. Select “OK” to confirm scale.

steps.title: Outline the well of interest using the circle drawing tool
steps.instruction: Outline the well of interest using the circle drawing tool.

steps.title: Adjust the contrast and brightness of...
steps.instruction: Adjust the contrast and brightness of the image so that the diagonal contrast line is just touching the edge of the side of peak on confocal images (Figure 3A) the top of the singular peak in Bio-Rad Chemdoc™ images (Figure 3B), or positioned immediately after the first of the two peaks in the smartphone images (Figure 3C). This is done by selecting the “Image” tab and then selecting the “Adjust” tab.

steps.title: Apply a 1
steps.instruction: Apply a 1.5% Gaussian blur. This is found in the “Process” tab under the “Filters” menu. This step allows ImageJ to smoothen the edges of the plaques and ignore the sharper edges in the plaques.

steps.title: Adjust the threshold to include ~2
steps.instruction: Adjust the threshold to include ~2.5%–5% of pixels (Figure 3D). Threshold parameters are set in the “Image” tab and then select the “Adjust” tab. This step allows for the most optimization to collected images. Use the mask available in ImageJ to adjust the threshold values until the mask overlaps with the plaque area as desired. There will be some areas of the threshold highlighted outside of the desired plaques due to some variation in cell uptake of stain. These small areas will be filtered out at a later step, optimizing full plaque coverage.

steps.title: In the “Process” tab, access the...
steps.instruction: In the “Process” tab, access the “Binary” menu and select “fill holes”.

steps.title: Select the “Analyze” tab and choose “Analyze particles”
steps.instruction: Select the “Analyze” tab and choose “Analyze particles”. Here set the lower size limit as needed to filter out background cell noise that did not meet the threshold requirements.

steps.title: Export data to Excel or other data processing applications
steps.instruction: Export data to Excel or other data processing applications. Data include plaque size and counts.

steps.title: Repeat the analysis with all of...
steps.instruction: Repeat the analysis with all of the collected images using parameters optimized on test image.

steps.title: Example histograms indicating how to apply the threshold
steps.instruction: Example histograms indicating how to apply the threshold. (A) Example histogram of contrast from confocal imaging with a diagonal line illustrating how to adjust contrast to the base of the curve. (B) Example histogram of contrast from Bio-Rad imager with a diagonal line illustrating how to adjust contrast to the peak of the curve. (C) Example histogram of contrast from smartphone imaging with a diagonal line illustrating how to adjust contrast to the middle of the bimodal curves. (D) Example positioning of threshold level.
